case_name: In the Matter of Andrew Evans and Zelda Keller

segments.text: Good afternoon. For the record, Justice Gould can't physically be with us here today, but he
will be reviewing a recording of the argument and will be deliberating and
participating in the decision of this case.
Attorney Friedman, I understand you reserved two minutes. Yes, thank you.
Please proceed. May I please the court? I'm Jesse Friedman
and I appear today for the petitioner, Andrew Evans.
This appeal asks whether a parent may defeat New Hampshire's child custody
jurisdiction by unilaterally removing three children
to the other side of the world without the other parent's knowledge
or consent just before New Hampshire
unequivocally would have had jurisdiction. The family division's order
should be reversed for three main reasons.
First, the court misapplied the UCCJEA
when it treated the children's time in New Hampshire
as a temporary absence from New Zealand, while refusing to treat the
respondent's unilateral January 2025 removal of the children from
New Hampshire as a temporary absence from New Hampshire.
You don't dispute that the family intended to live in
New Hampshire temporarily. So we believe that the family intended
to be in New Hampshire, to live in New Hampshire,
and there was no unequivocal plan to leave New Hampshire
certainly at the time that the respondent fled to New Zealand with the
three children. So it matters not what the original intent.
Mr. Friedman says that in July 2014 the parties decided to leave New Zealand
and stay temporarily with the respondent's parents in New Hampshire.
So I think it is true and there was evidence in the family court
that originally the family believed that they would return to New Zealand
but the character of the family's relationship changed in September of 2024.
So from that point on there was no longer a definitive plan
to return to New Zealand. I do not believe the original
initial intent matters. My position is, which I think is
laid out in the brief, that after December 27th
the petitioner had unequivocally let the respondent know that he did not consent
for the children to be removed to New Zealand
and he was no longer on board with the original plan.
And when I say unequivocally in December 27th of 2024,
I believe since September there was no longer a plan that either of them
could count on that it was the intent to return to New Zealand.
And I think the record is replete with references.
There was a mediation agreement, other memos between the parties
that's part of the record that had laid out other options other than returning
to New Zealand.
I think that's true but I think once the respondent knew
unequivocally that there was no consent for the children to leave the
country and being that while I understand that under the UCCJA
obviously another country can be a home state.
I think the fact that we're talking about American citizens
and the fact that we were talking about a petitioner
that also the record is replete with references that he did not know
if he was any longer going to be able to return to New Zealand
when the couple was no longer together.
So I do think and I am not contesting this court using a totality
of the circumstances test which I think is laid out in the
respondent's brief and I don't have an objection with that.
What I would point out is the first part of that test
does look toward the intent of the parties and while I do agree
that there would be a difference of the opposite in the scenario
that you bring up Justice Will I would suggest that in this case
that wasn't the case and therefore looking at the totality of the
circumstances it was clear to all the parties for months
that the petitioner was not necessarily on board
was going to New Zealand. I would point out that in the record which was
part of the respondent's appendix or attached to the brief
there are texts as late as January 13th of 2025 that indicated
that the respondent knew that the petitioner was not agreeing
to go back to New Zealand and that the respondent was actually
going to enroll the children in school in New Hampshire
and she had again reached out to the client I just want to be clear
you're saying that you're not agreeing for me to go to New Zealand
to enroll the children there and the answer I believe was correct.
Did they both get New Hampshire driver's licenses?
They did.
And when was that?
So I don't remember the exact month but I believe it was
August or September and I can tell you that there was testimony
in relation to them establishing residence for the purpose
of voting at that point that there was a petition in 2024
so they had come to New Hampshire they were living in New Hampton
I would point out and I think this is laid out this was not a random
stop along the way this was the respondent's parents house
they lived in New Hampton they had a camp on Lake Winnipesaki
in Bear Island the children had been coming to New Hampshire
for most of their lives and they would come here for extended periods
in the summer so it was not random that they were simply on the way
through New Hampshire back to New Zealand and then decided
not to go back and I do think it's important to point out so they
they had established in my opinion I had argued residency
in New Hampton they obtained New Hampshire's driver's licenses they
had obtained New Hampshire health insurance they were
going to the doctor and receiving mail at that New Hampton address so they
had done everything to avail themselves
of the the benefits of being New Hampshire citizens
here to vote here to live but then the respondent comes forward later and says
well this was all temporary and we were always going back
I do not believe as I said that the record supports that
certainly after the parties were no longer together in a relationship after
September. And is it your position that the New
Zealand court would not have qualified as
home state under the UCCPJ? So that is my position and I believe
that the court you know not only did the court
err in finding that New Hampshire was the home state I think they erred in
making a definitive finding that New Zealand was the home state and then
based upon that the court should have moved on
to the substantial connection test under 458.812.1b
which the court did not do in this case so first and foremost
my argument is that New Hampshire should have been the home state and the main
reason New Hampshire should have been the home state
is that whether it's eight or nine days that the respondent had left prior to
being here six months that the her leaving
without consent was constituted unjustifiable conduct
that the court should have made a far stronger adverse
inference against the respondent. So that
period of time after she left should have been looked at
as a temporary absence and her conduct should have
altered that. There was no evidence though that New Zealand had actually declined
jurisdiction is that correct? Correct and I do not believe at that time
it's my recollection of the record that there was not even a case in New
Zealand at that time.
So I think even the family court found that there was no consent
and that the respondent knew that the petitioner was not agreeing
again to let them leave and I think the court needs to understand
and I agree with what you're saying in certain cases that might be the
appropriate road for the court to go down. In this case
you need to look at the big picture you have all American citizens
that had only been in New Zealand for one school year.
This was not some other place that they always traveled
like New Hampshire was. There was evidence of a fellowship which I'll get
to in a moment because I believe that was one of the errors that the family
court made. But you have evidence through this
fellowship that it was only that period of time from the prior
school year that the family had ever been to New Zealand
and while there may have been some talk and some testimony
that the fellowship may or may not have led to a pathway of citizenship
for the family, that was unknown and there was
references both in the lower court order as well as in the testimony
that the petitioner did not believe that he could actually travel to New
Zealand after the respondent left. You will see in the record and attached
in my appendix the text messages after the
respondent had left and the petitioner's response was
oh my god you've kidnapped my children no I did not consent I did not consent.
So this is a situation where essentially there was the stability and
oversight in New Hampshire a place that everybody
had known to the respondent leaving knowing that she wasn't
there was no consent from the petitioner halfway across the world.
Can you briefly address the invocation of
Yes so I mean I will say I think there are three procedural errors which I think
I sort of see us all lumped together which I think in total were a
significant abuse of discretion from the family court
so and so first and foremost the respondent was allowed to appear by
Webex. In isolation I understand that the
court would have jurisdiction and that's not an issue in and of itself.
The problem was that at the time and there was evidence put forward in the
court which is in the transcript and was a finding that the court made
there were three felony warrants for interference with custody
that were outstanding against the respondent so she had been charged a
warrant had been issued by the New Hampton police department
and they were outstanding at the time so we had
objected to the Webex appearance because while we understand
again that it's allowed and that they cannot be
essentially trying to hold somebody accountable in the criminal realm in
a civil case in that circumstance it allowed her to use
Webex as a shield from service and the consequences what would have happened
had she returned to New Hampshire. What then made it even worse was
she was then sort of and ultimately allowed to invoke
the privilege against self-incrimination and I say sort of
because part of my issue with this and I objected throughout the transcript
is that there was some selective invocation at the beginning the
respondent takes the position in their briefs that this was
only in relation to one issue but she essentially there was all this talk of
invocation she was then sworn in and a very brief
a very brief offer by respondent's attorney
about this fellowship issue and then there continued to be
some evidence that was put forward by offer of proof
after she had invoked so was she put put on because
they were proceeding by offer of proof so she did not testify I had asked to be
able to cross-examine her and I strenuously object because they were
they were going to present an offer of proof correct
and so the whole hearing from from the beginning
went forward as a full evidentiary hearing the petitioner took the stand
so there was no offer the hearing started for about 10 seconds
by offer proof and then it turned out the petitioner took the stand to testify
for an extended period of time was subject to full cross-examination by
respondent's counsel so the the issue that we had
is this invocation first off it was it was clear
error for the court to allow any offer after an invocation because the
rules are clear obviously no confrontation at that point
family division rule 1.28 is clear you can do it person needs to be in the
courtroom but they need to be put on the road
and we'll continue this thank you you reserve two minutes turn your
dress call may please the court my name is rich
driscoll and i represent the respondent zelda keller
the petitioner's position is that he can unilaterally keep
the children here in new hampshire without a judicial order
despite the respondent's clear intent that she intended to return
to new zealand that is inconsistent with the language
and the structure of the ucc jea which has a mechanical
structure for determining whether jurisdiction applies
the ucc jea confers jurisdiction in new hampshire
if new hampshire was the child's home state when the action commenced or if
it was the home state within the six months before the action was commenced
to be a home state the children must live in the state

but are you saying that new zealand therefore was the home state at that

the last home state that the children had they moved there in i believe it was 2023
they were there for consecutive months

there can be no doubt that when the children moved to new zealand they

and it did become their home state uh at some point in i believe it was

and some things change between the parties where
there's doubts as to whether the petitioner wants to return
but when they when they came back to new hampshire it was their intent

so getting a driver's license has it's more than just being able to vote like

i'm not sure if the record reflects this but it's my recollection

there was evidence that they told the school administrators in new zealand

wasn't there evidence they had a bunch of stuff in hawaii too?
that's that is correct but they hadn't there was
they had not been to hawaii in a few years since
then so just having stopped her isn't the test
it's not but it's a factor that should be considered under the totality of
the circumstances that's right judge will but
the intent this is another another factor
this is why the totality of the circumstances test is the is the best
test in this in this scenario because the trial court can consider the party's

intents changed over time and so while the
the respondents intent never changed you know the petitioners did and that

so no your honor did she register to vote here
i believe so your honor and she did vote i i don't know but presumably if she

and the fact that they weren't here for those nine days defeats jurisdiction
so there are cases that support the proposition

can defeat jurisdiction but that's only here in new hampshire
and so the cases that support this are typically

and then refuses to return them to the other parent
on the you know regular schedule and then files an action here in new

and rsa 458 a 19 expressly says like the courts must decline

well no your honor because the the the statute doesn't contemplate
um avoiding jurisdiction by one parent like leaving

isn't an absolute is it because why did you have

to determining like new hampshire cannot be the home state because they were not

and so if the court doesn't have the opportunity to evaluate the

i'm not sure they would be able to physically leave without the passports

yes your honor and even though um this is not

if new hampshire is the home state it's an un if new hampshire is not the

that doesn't change the analysis going forward if the court included that

to leave it doesn't really change the analysis because it goes to the

is not the home state and because new zealand is the home state
the trial court properly concluded that it lacked jurisdiction

thank you thank you attorney to school attorney freeman
so just a a couple brief responses and continuing some of where i left off

that's not true the definition of home state includes temporary

leads to that any absence prior to the six months
would have instilled jurisdiction in new hampshire
should have been deemed a temporary absence
chief justice mcdonald i just want to say that not only did the petitioner

initial_prompt: This is a New Hampshire Supreme Court oral argument. Preserve legal terminology, party names, docket numbers, and court names. Likely speakers include justices and counsel.
text:
Good afternoon. For the record, Justice Gould can't physically be with us here today, but he

will be reviewing a recording of the argument and will be deliberating and

participating in the decision of this case.

Attorney Friedman, I understand you reserved two minutes. Yes, thank you.

Please proceed. May I please the court? I'm Jesse Friedman

and I appear today for the petitioner, Andrew Evans.

This appeal asks whether a parent may defeat New Hampshire's child custody

jurisdiction by unilaterally removing three children

to the other side of the world without the other parent's knowledge

or consent just before New Hampshire

unequivocally would have had jurisdiction. The family division's order

should be reversed for three main reasons.

First, the court misapplied the UCCJEA

when it treated the children's time in New Hampshire

as a temporary absence from New Zealand, while refusing to treat the

respondent's unilateral January 2025 removal of the children from

New Hampshire as a temporary absence from New Hampshire.

You don't dispute that the family intended to live in

New Hampshire temporarily. So we believe that the family intended

to be in New Hampshire, to live in New Hampshire,

and there was no unequivocal plan to leave New Hampshire

certainly at the time that the respondent fled to New Zealand with the

three children. So it matters not what the original intent.

Mr. Friedman says that in July 2014 the parties decided to leave New Zealand

and stay temporarily with the respondent's parents in New Hampshire.

So I think it is true and there was evidence in the family court

that originally the family believed that they would return to New Zealand

but the character of the family's relationship changed in September of 2024.

So from that point on there was no longer a definitive plan

to return to New Zealand. I do not believe the original

initial intent matters. My position is, which I think is

laid out in the brief, that after December 27th

the petitioner had unequivocally let the respondent know that he did not consent

for the children to be removed to New Zealand

and he was no longer on board with the original plan.

And when I say unequivocally in December 27th of 2024,

I believe since September there was no longer a plan that either of them

could count on that it was the intent to return to New Zealand.

And I think the record is replete with references.

There was a mediation agreement, other memos between the parties

that's part of the record that had laid out other options other than returning

to New Zealand.

I think that's true but I think once the respondent knew

unequivocally that there was no consent for the children to leave the

country and being that while I understand that under the UCCJA

obviously another country can be a home state.

I think the fact that we're talking about American citizens

and the fact that we were talking about a petitioner

that also the record is replete with references that he did not know

if he was any longer going to be able to return to New Zealand

when the couple was no longer together.

So I do think and I am not contesting this court using a totality

of the circumstances test which I think is laid out in the

respondent's brief and I don't have an objection with that.

What I would point out is the first part of that test

does look toward the intent of the parties and while I do agree

that there would be a difference of the opposite in the scenario

that you bring up Justice Will I would suggest that in this case

that wasn't the case and therefore looking at the totality of the

circumstances it was clear to all the parties for months

that the petitioner was not necessarily on board

was going to New Zealand. I would point out that in the record which was

part of the respondent's appendix or attached to the brief

there are texts as late as January 13th of 2025 that indicated

that the respondent knew that the petitioner was not agreeing

to go back to New Zealand and that the respondent was actually

going to enroll the children in school in New Hampshire

and she had again reached out to the client I just want to be clear

you're saying that you're not agreeing for me to go to New Zealand

to enroll the children there and the answer I believe was correct.

Did they both get New Hampshire driver's licenses?

They did.

And when was that?

So I don't remember the exact month but I believe it was

August or September and I can tell you that there was testimony

in relation to them establishing residence for the purpose

of voting at that point that there was a petition in 2024

so they had come to New Hampshire they were living in New Hampton

I would point out and I think this is laid out this was not a random

stop along the way this was the respondent's parents house

they lived in New Hampton they had a camp on Lake Winnipesaki

in Bear Island the children had been coming to New Hampshire

for most of their lives and they would come here for extended periods

in the summer so it was not random that they were simply on the way

through New Hampshire back to New Zealand and then decided

not to go back and I do think it's important to point out so they

they had established in my opinion I had argued residency

in New Hampton they obtained New Hampshire's driver's licenses they

had obtained New Hampshire health insurance they were

going to the doctor and receiving mail at that New Hampton address so they

had done everything to avail themselves

of the the benefits of being New Hampshire citizens

here to vote here to live but then the respondent comes forward later and says

well this was all temporary and we were always going back

I do not believe as I said that the record supports that

certainly after the parties were no longer together in a relationship after

September. And is it your position that the New

Zealand court would not have qualified as

home state under the UCCPJ? So that is my position and I believe

that the court you know not only did the court

err in finding that New Hampshire was the home state I think they erred in

making a definitive finding that New Zealand was the home state and then

based upon that the court should have moved on

to the substantial connection test under 458.812.1b

which the court did not do in this case so first and foremost

my argument is that New Hampshire should have been the home state and the main

reason New Hampshire should have been the home state

is that whether it's eight or nine days that the respondent had left prior to

being here six months that the her leaving

without consent was constituted unjustifiable conduct

that the court should have made a far stronger adverse

inference against the respondent. So that

period of time after she left should have been looked at

as a temporary absence and her conduct should have

altered that. There was no evidence though that New Zealand had actually declined

jurisdiction is that correct? Correct and I do not believe at that time

it's my recollection of the record that there was not even a case in New

Zealand at that time.

So I think even the family court found that there was no consent

and that the respondent knew that the petitioner was not agreeing

again to let them leave and I think the court needs to understand

and I agree with what you're saying in certain cases that might be the

appropriate road for the court to go down. In this case

you need to look at the big picture you have all American citizens

that had only been in New Zealand for one school year.

This was not some other place that they always traveled

like New Hampshire was. There was evidence of a fellowship which I'll get

to in a moment because I believe that was one of the errors that the family

court made. But you have evidence through this

fellowship that it was only that period of time from the prior

school year that the family had ever been to New Zealand

and while there may have been some talk and some testimony

that the fellowship may or may not have led to a pathway of citizenship

for the family, that was unknown and there was

references both in the lower court order as well as in the testimony

that the petitioner did not believe that he could actually travel to New

Zealand after the respondent left. You will see in the record and attached

in my appendix the text messages after the

respondent had left and the petitioner's response was

oh my god you've kidnapped my children no I did not consent I did not consent.

So this is a situation where essentially there was the stability and

oversight in New Hampshire a place that everybody

had known to the respondent leaving knowing that she wasn't

there was no consent from the petitioner halfway across the world.

Can you briefly address the invocation of

Yes so I mean I will say I think there are three procedural errors which I think

I sort of see us all lumped together which I think in total were a

significant abuse of discretion from the family court

so and so first and foremost the respondent was allowed to appear by

Webex. In isolation I understand that the

court would have jurisdiction and that's not an issue in and of itself.

The problem was that at the time and there was evidence put forward in the

court which is in the transcript and was a finding that the court made

there were three felony warrants for interference with custody

that were outstanding against the respondent so she had been charged a

warrant had been issued by the New Hampton police department

and they were outstanding at the time so we had

objected to the Webex appearance because while we understand

again that it's allowed and that they cannot be

essentially trying to hold somebody accountable in the criminal realm in

a civil case in that circumstance it allowed her to use

Webex as a shield from service and the consequences what would have happened

had she returned to New Hampshire. What then made it even worse was

she was then sort of and ultimately allowed to invoke

the privilege against self-incrimination and I say sort of

because part of my issue with this and I objected throughout the transcript

is that there was some selective invocation at the beginning the

respondent takes the position in their briefs that this was
